title: Consumer contracts
text: Consumer contracts may choose a governing law, but that choice cannot deprive the consumer of mandatory protections of the law of the consumer’s habitual residence when the trader directs activities to that country. In the absence of choice, the law of the consumer’s habitual residence typically applies.

title: Employment contracts
text: An individual employment contract may have a chosen law, but mandatory protections of the employee’s habitual place of work prevail. Without choice, the law of the country where the employee habitually works generally applies.

title: General rule for contracts
text: If no law is chosen, the contract is governed by the law of the country most closely connected, often the place of the characteristic performance, unless there is a manifestly closer connection to another country.

title: General rule for torts
text: Non-contractual obligations are generally governed by the law of the country where the damage occurs. If both parties share a common habitual residence, that law applies; otherwise a manifestly closer connection may displace the general rule.

title: Jurisdiction—contracts
text: In matters relating to a contract, a person domiciled in a Member State may be sued in the courts for the place of performance of the obligation in question (with specific rules for sales of goods and services).

title: Jurisdiction—torts
text: In matters relating to tort, a person may be sued in the courts for the place where the harmful event occurred or may occur (place of event giving rise to damage or place of damage).
